behaviors.description: Command segmentation treats newline-separated statements as separate segments, not one monolithic block.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in parsing/splitting logic — a single function that tokenizes a string on newlines. The bug lives entirely within one algorithmic boundary; no module handoff is required for a test to catch a wrong segment count or wrong segment content. REAL-INFRA: No filesystem, subprocess, or network involved. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected — there is no cross-module boundary in segmentation alone. The function takes a string and returns segments; a unit test with a multi-line input string is sufficient to expose the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Call the segmentation function with a string containing two newline-separated assignments followed by a `gh pr create` invocation. Assert the output is a list of three separate segments, not one monolithic string. Also assert that a single-line command produces exactly one segment.

behaviors.description: A multi-line shell block with variable assignments followed by `gh pr create` is recognized as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The incident bug was precisely that segmentation was NOT wired into detection — 'the hook classified the whole block as not_a_trigger' because it evaluated the unsegmented blob. A unit test of the detection function alone (fed pre-segmented input) would pass even if the real pipeline never calls segmentation first. The failure only surfaces at the boundary between the segmentation stage and the trigger-classification stage. REAL-INFRA: No OS or network needed; an in-process test of the wired pipeline suffices. REJECTION-GATE: Unit was considered; disqualifying evidence is the incident description itself — detection ran on the monolithic block, meaning the bug is at the segment→classify handoff, not within either stage alone.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a raw multi-line shell block string containing two variable-assignment lines followed by `gh pr create --title 'x' --body 'y'`. Pass the raw block through the full detection pipeline (segmentation → trigger classification). Assert the returned classification is a PR-create trigger, not `not_a_trigger`.

behaviors.description: A similar multi-line block that ends with `gh issue create` is not incorrectly treated as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The key assertion is that the pattern matcher correctly distinguishes `gh issue create` from `gh pr create`. There is no `gh pr create` anywhere in the input, so no cross-module wiring error can produce a false positive — the failure mode is purely within the pattern-matching logic. A unit test on the classifier (or the full pipeline fed a clearly non-matching string) is sufficient to expose any false-positive pattern. REAL-INFRA: None required. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered because behavior 2 is Integration, but the failure mode differs — false negatives (behavior 2) require segmentation+detection to be wired; false positives (behavior 3) require only that the pattern does not over-match, which unit input alone can verify.
behaviors.test_description: Pass a multi-line block containing variable assignments and `gh issue create` through the detection pipeline (or directly to the classifier). Assert the result is `not_a_trigger` and is not classified as a PR-create event. Parameterize with variants: `gh issue create`, `gh issue list`, bare `gh` with no subcommand.

behaviors.description: When a PR-create trigger is detected, the hook can reconstruct and persist the PR identifier needed by the review loop state.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: 'Persist' involves real filesystem writes; the behavior asserts a state file is created with the correct content. REAL-INFRA: A real temporary directory is needed — if the persistence code has path construction errors or wrong serialization format, a mock filesystem would silently accept them. An in-process real temp dir (not OS-level daemon) is sufficient; this stays Integration, not System. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking `open()` or the file path would always succeed regardless of path construction bugs. LLM-DEP: None. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered but rejected — there is no subprocess, real network, or OS-daemon dependency; a real temp dir within the test process catches all relevant failures.
behaviors.test_description: Given a detected PR-create trigger containing a PR number or URL, call the persistence function with a real temp directory as the state directory. Assert the state file exists at the expected path and its contents deserialize to a structure containing the correct PR identifier. Test both a numeric PR ID and a full URL form to cover reconstruction edge cases.

behaviors.description: After PR-create detection, the review gate state is written in a form that the pre-tool enforcement hook can read and enforce.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This explicitly spans two hook modules — the PostToolUse writer and the pre-tool enforcement reader. The failure mode is a format mismatch between what is written and what is read; this only appears when both modules run against the same real file. Neither module alone can reveal this. REAL-INFRA: A shared real temp directory is needed to expose schema or path disagreements between the two hooks. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the reader or writer in isolation means each test sees only its own expectations; the contract between them stays untested. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered (hooks might run as subprocesses in production) but rejected — in-process calls to both hook modules against a real temp dir reproduce the format-contract failure without needing a real subprocess. If the hooks are only invocable as subprocesses, escalate to System.
behaviors.test_description: In a shared real temp directory: (1) invoke the PostToolUse hook's state-write path with a synthetic PR-create trigger, (2) invoke the pre-tool enforcement hook's state-read path against the same directory. Assert the enforcement hook reads a non-empty gate state and would block further tool use. Assert that if the directory is empty (no prior PR creation), the enforcement hook does not block.
